title: What is the difference between ArrayList and LinkedList?

answers.junior: ArrayList and LinkedList both implement the List interface but differ fundamentally in their underlying data structures and performance characteristics. ArrayList uses a dynamic array internally, starting with a default capacity of 10 and growing by 50% (newCapacity = oldCapacity + (oldCapacity >> 1)) when resized, which makes random access O(1) via index but insertions or deletions in the middle O(n) due to element shifting.
answers.middle: ArrayList and LinkedList both implement the List interface but differ fundamentally in their underlying data structures and performance characteristics. ArrayList uses a dynamic array internally, starting with a default capacity of 10 and growing by 50% (newCapacity = oldCapacity + (oldCapacity >> 1)) when resized, which makes random access O(1) via index but insertions or deletions in the middle O(n) due to element shifting. LinkedList uses a doubly-linked list of node objects, each holding a reference to previous and next nodes, making insertions and deletions at known positions O(1), but random access O(n) because traversal from head or tail is required. Memory layout is a critical real-world distinction: ArrayList stores elements contiguously in heap memory, giving excellent CPU cache locality and prefetch behavior, while LinkedList stores scattered node objects that result in cache misses and significantly higher per-element overhead (each node carries two object references plus the element). In practice, ArrayList often outperforms LinkedList even for insertions due to this cache advantage, which is why LinkedList is rarely the optimal choice in modern JVM applications.
answers.senior: ArrayList and LinkedList both implement the List interface but differ fundamentally in their underlying data structures and performance characteristics. ArrayList uses a dynamic array internally, starting with a default capacity of 10 and growing by 50% (newCapacity = oldCapacity + (oldCapacity >> 1)) when resized, which makes random access O(1) via index but insertions or deletions in the middle O(n) due to element shifting. LinkedList uses a doubly-linked list of node objects, each holding a reference to previous and next nodes, making insertions and deletions at known positions O(1), but random access O(n) because traversal from head or tail is required. Memory layout is a critical real-world distinction: ArrayList stores elements contiguously in heap memory, giving excellent CPU cache locality and prefetch behavior, while LinkedList stores scattered node objects that result in cache misses and significantly higher per-element overhead (each node carries two object references plus the element). In practice, ArrayList often outperforms LinkedList even for insertions due to this cache advantage, which is why LinkedList is rarely the optimal choice in modern JVM applications. LinkedList also implements the Deque interface, making it suitable as a double-ended queue with O(1) addFirst, addLast, removeFirst, and removeLast operations — this is its primary legitimate use case over ArrayList. Neither is thread-safe; for concurrent access use CopyOnWriteArrayList for read-heavy workloads or Collections.synchronizedList() as a simple wrapper. ArrayList.subList() returns a live view backed by the original list, meaning modifications to the sublist affect the original, which can be a subtle bug source. In production, ArrayList is almost always the default choice; LinkedList should be preferred only when the implementation requires frequent insertion/removal at both ends and the Deque API is actively needed.

Neither is thread-safe; for concurrent access use CopyOnWriteArrayList for read-heavy workloads or Collections.synchronizedList() as a simple wrapper. In production, ArrayList is almost always the default choice; LinkedList should be preferred only when the implementation requires frequent insertion/removal at both ends and the Deque API is actively needed.
